item.title: When did not aspiring to have kids become so taboo?
item.description: TLDR: Stop telling people they are immature or they're crazy or anything else insulting for choosing not to have kids. I'm almost 30. From the moment I got married at 24, my mother has been asking for grand babies. I never ever wanted kids, I knew this since I was 15. The world is chaotic, it's not sustainable, there is overpopulation, and living is fucking hard. Not to mention the medical risk it is for anyone to give birth, or the cost it would be to have a surrogate, or literally having a child out of moral obligation when you're not mentally prepared and how unfair that is to everyone in any situation. I'm not shittin on people who make it work and literally find their joy and purpose when they have a child, because I know being afraid and not wanting a baby can change when they're born. I know that happens. I have a friend who got pregnant early and I love her son, I'm so proud of her, there are literal super people out there doing this against all odds. It's just, if I chose not to have a child, how does that make me immature? If I die, my money, whatever little there is left because I plan on fucking living, won't get passed down, we won't leave a legacy, we won't continue our family bloodline etc etc. Why is that all important? I just wanna fucking live man, travel and for once not feel like I'm in a motherfuckin box of a life chained to money and obligations simply because society said that's 'normal'. Anyways. It was bothering me cuz posts like that were increasing lately, then I kept seeing El*n having his too many children and talking about repopulation because of the decline in births. But why on this dying overpopulated planet is that a fucking concern?
item.dc:creator: SaffronGin

item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: "We start talking to this young white lady, and she invites us to her room upstairs. We start making out on her bed when her roommate walks in. She starts yelling at the girl we were with: `You're having sex on my bed? You're a slut!'"
item.description: "From there, the reigns of racism took over. The young lady accused us of raping her. Going to court, facing an all-white jury, all I remember is how terrified my mom was. She said, `Baby, you don't understand. This ain't about the truth. This is about the reality of the America we live in.' "I was sentenced to 28 years in prison based off an accusation, and nothing more. My stomach is in knots just thinking about how easy it was for one accusation to be enough to turn my life upside down. I was 18 turning 19. I lost my innocence and youth that day. "Prison was a dark time for me, but I found strength from my mom. She couldn't afford to hire a lawyer for me, so she felt like it was her fault I was found guilty. "I wanted to rewrite what had been written about me. I started studying case law in the prison's library, and also helped other people with their cases — that was my crash course on law school. I wrote to people, and the Innocence Project answered. "We took the case to the Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals, and it was unanimously reversed. The case unraveled because we discovered that police withheld a statement from a college student, who said he saw us hanging out with our accuser before and after we went up to her room." "A more equitable criminal justice system looks like the diverse picture of America that we have. You can't have benches made up of 90% white former prosecutors. When I was standing in that courtroom with the all-white jury looking at me — we did not get the presumption of innocence. We went in with the historical stereotype of aggressive, ravaging, savaging Black boys. "One of the most significant cases I'm working on is US v. Claiborne. Two men, Ferrone Claiborne and Terence Richardson, were wrongfully charged with the murder of a police officer and sentenced to life — even though the jury found them not guilty. "The court had the opportunity to protect the Constitution or the police. And the court decided to protect the police. Protecting bad police erodes our faith in the entire police force. This country has grappled with qualified immunity, like with Breonna Taylor. We too often have judges and decisions that protect their positions and their party, but not the Constitution. "They call the prison system the Department of Corrections, but it really should be called the Department of Warehousing. Millions of Black men are incarcerated. Neighborhoods are suffering — it's like a leg missing from a chair, when you have that many men missing. If it really were a place for corrections, there would be change. But we're not providing mental healthcare or education to make sure people coming in and out of prison have the resources they need. We're warehousing them. "I think we need to treat things that ail our society as the pandemics they are: We have a pandemic of civil rights of Black folks in this world. We have a pandemic of women's rights, a pandemic when it comes to the justice system. We all need to stop and mobilize together and figure out how we get out of these pandemics together." https://www.insider.com/jarrett-adams-criminal-defense-lawyer-racial-justice-diversity-2022-7

item.title: Quitting smoking is a wild ride
item.description: Been a pack and a half a day for 17 years. Quitting because the pulmonologist thinks I might have COPD. No one told me how much everything smells. Food also tastes way too rich so I’m getting used to that

item.title: My garden - was mud a year ago. Adapted for my sons wheelchair.
item.description:
My morning coffee spot #greenthumb
Lupins from seed
Beautiful dahlia from tuber
It’s packed, but I can see a gap - then I can buy a plant!
I made these using my own photos
More cushions I made using my own photos - put through a watercolour filter
Night time vibes
Doggo and food stop a few months ago so a bit bare
Sweet peas!
Smelly roses for my son
Lots of bees this year and lots of flowers to come
A packed border
Lots of nice smells near my sons daybed
Then before photo
item.dc:creator: daffodilpies

item.title: Numerous people have had several issues with her over the last couple of years. It just sucks to know I’m gonna get someone fired.
item.description: He was out of line, threw her phone and food at me, made a scene in front of guests, snatched a product out of my possession. She said, “You’re really passing me of today.” She was extremely aggressive and scary.
item.dc:creator: PoptartSpankySpoogeMonkey

item.title: This was my brother, ca. 1979
item.description: We lost him two days ago. He was my best friend and the only one I confided in. I always liked this photo best. I’m gonna miss you, Rick.
item.dc:creator: OctopusStinkhorn
